 {"id":"ntm-161","title":"Implement ntm mail inbox command","description":"# Implement ntm mail inbox Command

## Purpose

Display an AGGREGATE inbox view showing messages across ALL agents in the project, not just NTM's coordinator messages.

## IMPORTANT: Aggregate View

This is not just NTM's inbox. It shows:
1. Messages TO any agent in the project (GreenCastle, BlueLake, etc.)
2. Messages FROM any agent in the project
3. Filtered by session agents if desired

This gives the user visibility into all agent-to-agent communication.

## Command Syntax

```bash
# Show all messages in project (across all agents)
ntm mail inbox myproject

# Filter to session's agents only
ntm mail inbox myproject --session-agents

# Filter to specific agent
ntm mail inbox myproject --agent GreenCastle

# Urgent only
ntm mail inbox myproject --urgent

# JSON for scripts
ntm mail inbox myproject --json
```

## Output Format

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Project Inbox: myproject (5 agents)                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● #42 [URGENT] API Review Needed                            │
│   GreenCastle → BlueLake                                    │
│   2 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123 │ ⚠️ Ack required         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #41 Tests passing, ready for merge                        │
│   RedStone → GreenCastle, BlueLake                          │
│   15 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #40 Starting work on src/api/**                           │
│   ntm_myproject → all                                       │
│   30 minutes ago │ (coordinator announcement)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Showing messages involving: GreenCastle, BlueLake, RedStone, ntm_myproject
```

## Implementation

1. Discover all agents in project via Agent Mail
2. Fetch inbox for EACH agent
3. Deduplicate (same message may appear in multiple inboxes)
4. Sort by timestamp
5. Display with sender → recipient format

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Shows messages across ALL project agents
- [ ] Deduplication (message shown once even if multiple recipients)
- [ ] Filter by --session-agents, --agent, --urgent
- [ ] Clear indication of sender → recipient
- [ ] Coordinator messages distinguished from agent messages

 {"id":"ntm-1ts","title":"Implement ntm lock/unlock file reservation commands","description":"# Implement ntm lock/unlock File Reservation Commands

## Purpose

Expose Agent Mail's file reservation system as NTM commands, allowing agents to declare intent before editing files and avoid conflicts.

## Command Syntax

```bash
# Reserve files for editing
ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" --reason "Implementing user endpoints"

# Reserve multiple patterns
ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" "tests/api/**"

# With TTL (default 1 hour)
ntm lock myproject "config/*.json" --ttl 2h

# Shared (read) vs exclusive (write) lock
ntm lock myproject "docs/**" --shared

# Release specific locks
ntm unlock myproject "src/api/**"

# Release all locks for session
ntm unlock myproject --all

# Show current locks
ntm locks myproject
ntm locks myproject --all-agents  # Include other sessions
```

## Lock Display Format

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ File Reservations: myproject                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 🔒 src/api/**                                               │
│   Agent: ntm_myproject │ Exclusive │ Expires in 45m        │
│   Reason: Implementing user endpoints                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 🔒 tests/api/**                                             │
│   Agent: ntm_myproject │ Exclusive │ Expires in 45m        │
│   Reason: Implementing user endpoints                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 📖 docs/** (shared)                                         │
│   Agent: GreenCastle │ Shared │ Expires in 1h30m           │
│   Reason: Updating API documentation                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Conflict Detection

When attempting to lock:
```
⚠️ Conflict detected:
  Pattern: src/api/**
  Held by: GreenCastle (cc_1)
  Reason: Refactoring auth module
  Expires in: 25 minutes

Options:
  1. Wait for expiry
  2. Request release: ntm mail send myproject --to cc_1 "Need src/api/**"
  3. Force lock (dangerous): ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" --force
```

## Integration with spawn/add

When spawning agents:
```bash
# Check for conflicts before spawning
ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --check-locks

# Auto-reserve working directory
ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --reserve-cwd
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Lock patterns with glob support
- [ ] TTL specification (default 1h, max 24h)
- [ ] Exclusive vs shared locks
- [ ] Conflict detection with helpful messages
- [ ] ntm locks command shows current state
- [ ] --all-agents shows all project reservations
- [ ] Integration hooks for spawn command","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:42.508839-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:42.508839-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-1ts","depends_on_id":"ntm-yxa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:56.109325-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-20n","title":"Implement --robot-assign for work distribution recommendations","description":"AI-assisted work assignment suggestions based on current state.

## Rationale
With N agents and M ready beads, optimal assignment is non-trivial. This flag analyzes current state and recommends which agent should work on what, considering:
- Agent types and their strengths
- Current workload balance  
- Bead dependencies and priorities
- Recent activity patterns

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-assign myproject
ntm --robot-assign myproject --beads=ntm-abc,ntm-def  # specific beads
ntm --robot-assign myproject --strategy=balanced      # or: speed, quality
```

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "strategy": "balanced",
  "recommendations": [
    {
      "agent": "0.1",
      "agent_type": "claude",
      "assign_bead": "ntm-1rz",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "reasoning": "Profiler task matches claude's code analysis strengths"
    },
    {
      "agent": "0.2", 
      "agent_type": "codex",
      "assign_bead": "ntm-7mg",
      "confidence": 0.72,
      "reasoning": "UI/styling task, codex handles CSS well"
    }
  ],
  "blocked_beads": [
    {"id": "ntm-xyz", "blocked_by": ["ntm-abc"]}
  ],
  "idle_agents": ["0.3"],
  "unassignable_beads": [
    {"id": "ntm-qqq", "reason": "Requires human input"}
  ]
}
```

## Assignment Strategies
- balanced: distribute work evenly across agents
- speed: assign to fastest/most active agents  
- quality: match task types to agent strengths
- dependency: prioritize unblocking work

## Integration with Beads
Requires parsing bd ready and bd show output to understand task graph. Consider caching bead state to avoid repeated queries.

## Future Enhancement
Could integrate with agent-mail to check agent's stated preferences or current focus.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:51:50.71632-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T02:51:50.71632-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-a8q","title":"Implement --robot-ack for send confirmation","description":"Track and confirm message receipt by agents.

## Rationale
After --robot-send, I need to know:
1. Did the agent receive the message?
2. Did the agent acknowledge understanding?
3. Is the agent actively working on it?

Without this, I'm blind after sending - I have to poll tail output and parse for confirmation patterns.

## Proposal: Ack Tracking
When sending with --robot-send --track, ntm:
1. Watches pane output for acknowledgment patterns
2. Returns structured confirmation

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "sent_at": "ISO8601",
  "confirmations": [
    {
      "pane": "0.1",
      "ack_type": "prompt_returned",  // agent showed ready prompt
      "ack_at": "ISO8601",
      "latency_ms": 234
    },
    {
      "pane": "0.2",
      "ack_type": "echo_detected",    // agent echoed input
      "ack_at": "ISO8601",
      "latency_ms": 156
    }
  ],
  "pending": ["0.3"],  // still waiting
  "failed": []
}
```

## Ack Detection Patterns
- prompt_returned: Agent shows ready prompt after processing
- echo_detected: Input echoed in pane (basic confirmation)
- explicit_ack: Agent responds with understood/acknowledged
- output_started: Agent begins producing output

## Timeout Handling
--timeout=30s - wait up to 30s for acks
Returns partial result with pending list if timeout","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:58:03.223434-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T02:58:03.223434-05:00"}

NTM should eat its own dog food by using UBS to scan its own Go codebase, demonstrating the integration and catching real issues.

Per AGENTS.md: 'ubs <changed-files> before every commit. Exit 0 = safe.'

- Catches bugs in NTM's Go code
- Provides example output for documentation
- Builds confidence in the tooling

- Add UBS scan step to any CI/CD pipeline
- Fail PR if critical issues found
- Cache UBS installation for speed

- Triage and fix any critical/error findings
- Document false positives for future reference

 {"id":"ntm-ctt","title":"Add --json support to spawn, create, add, send commands","description":"Implement JSON output for commands that perform actions.

## What This Task Does

Add --json support to commands that modify state or perform actions:
- ntm spawn: Return session details after creation
- ntm create: Return session info after creation  
- ntm add: Return pane info for newly added agents
- ntm send: Return delivery confirmation for sent prompts

## Why These Commands

These commands are commonly used in scripts and automation:
- CI/CD pipelines spawn sessions and need session IDs
- Scripts add agents and need pane indices
- Automated workflows send prompts and need confirmation

## JSON Schemas

### ntm spawn --json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "created": true,
  "working_directory": "/path/to/project",
  "panes": [
    {"index": 0, "agent_type": "claude", "title": "myproject__cc_1"},
    {"index": 1, "agent_type": "codex", "title": "myproject__cod_1"}
  ],
  "agent_counts": {"claude": 2, "codex": 1}
}

### ntm send --json  
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "prompt_preview": "First 50 chars of prompt...",
  "targets": [0, 1, 2],
  "delivered": 3,
  "failed": 0
}

## Implementation

1. Create output structs for each command
2. Modify command to build struct before any output
3. Use output formatter based on --json flag
4. Ensure errors also return valid JSON when flag is set

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ntm spawn --json returns session details
- [ ] ntm create --json returns session info
- [ ] ntm add --json returns new pane info
- [ ] ntm send --json returns delivery confirmation
- [ ] Errors produce valid JSON with error field

-{"id":"ntm-cyq","title":"Implement token estimation library","description":"# Implement Token Estimation Library

## Purpose

Provide rough token estimates for context usage visualization.

## IMPORTANT: Estimation, Not Measurement

We're ESTIMATING tokens, not measuring actual usage. Be honest about this.

## Approach: Character-Based Heuristic

Simpler and more honest than pretending to have accurate tokenizers:

```go
package tokens

// EstimateTokens provides a rough token count estimate.
// Uses ~3.5 characters per token heuristic for English text.
// This is an ESTIMATE - actual token counts vary by model and content.
func EstimateTokens(text string) int {
    // ~3.5 chars per token for typical English
    return len(text) * 10 / 35
}

// EstimateWithOverhead applies overhead multiplier for hidden context.
// Overhead includes: system prompts, tool definitions, conversation structure.
func EstimateWithOverhead(visibleText string, multiplier float64) int {
    visible := EstimateTokens(visibleText)
    return int(float64(visible) * multiplier)
}
```

## Model Context Limits

Approximate limits (may change, conservative estimates):
```go
var ContextLimits = map[string]int{
    "opus":   200000,
    "sonnet": 200000,
    "haiku":  200000,
    "gpt4":   128000,
    "o1":     128000,
    "pro":    1000000,
    "flash":  1000000,
}

func GetContextLimit(model string) int {
    if limit, ok := ContextLimits[normalizeModel(model)]; ok {
        return limit
    }
    return 128000 // Conservative default
}
```

## Why Not Use tiktoken?

1. tiktoken is for OpenAI tokenizers - Claude uses different tokenization
2. Each model family has different tokenizers
3. Character heuristic is 80% accurate and works for ALL models
4. We're showing estimates anyway - precision is false confidence

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] EstimateTokens function with character heuristic
- [ ] EstimateWithOverhead for total context estimate
- [ ] Context limit lookup by model
- [ ] Clear documentation that these are estimates
- [ ] Unit tests validating reasonable estimates","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"SapphireRiver","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:08.230758-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:57:25.792561-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-czy","title":"EPIC: Remote Session Support","description":"Enable NTM to manage agents on remote machines via SSH.

## Problem Statement

AI coding agents are resource-intensive:
- High CPU usage during inference
- Significant memory for context windows
- Can slow down local development machines
- Laptop fans spinning constantly

Users want to run agents on powerful remote machines while keeping
their local environment responsive. Tmux already supports remote
sessions, but NTM doesn't expose this capability.

## Goals

1. Add --ssh flag to spawn, list, attach, send, status, kill
2. Seamlessly manage remote sessions as if they were local
3. Support SSH config hosts (~/.ssh/config)
4. Handle connection failures gracefully
5. Remember remote host association for sessions

## Why This Matters

Power users have access to:
- Cloud VMs with high CPU/memory
- Development servers with fast internet
- Dedicated machines for agent workloads
- GPU instances for local LLM agents

NTM should leverage these resources without requiring users to
manually SSH and run commands.

## Success Criteria

- ntm spawn myproject --ssh devbox --cc=4
- ntm list --ssh devbox
- ntm attach myproject --ssh devbox
- ntm send myproject --ssh devbox --all 'status'
- ntm status myproject --ssh devbox --json
- ntm kill myproject --ssh devbox

## Technical Approach

SSH wrapper for tmux commands:
- Local: tmux new-session -d -s myproject
- Remote: ssh devbox tmux new-session -d -s myproject

Connection management:
- Use user's SSH config and keys
- Support ControlMaster for connection reuse
- Timeout and retry on connection failures
- Clear error messages for auth failures

Session metadata:
- Store remote host with session (in tmux environment variable)
- Auto-detect remote host when operating on existing sessions
- Allow explicit override with --ssh flag

## Considerations

SSH Key Management:
- Rely on user's existing SSH setup
- Don't store passwords or implement auth
- Support ssh-agent for key passphrase caching

Network Reliability:
- Graceful handling of disconnects
- Retry logic for transient failures
- Clear feedback on connection status

Performance:
- Each command requires SSH connection (acceptable)
- Consider ControlMaster recommendation for users
- Batch operations where possible

## Interaction with Other Epics

- Session Persistence (ntm-bh5): Saved sessions include remote host
- Health Checks: Health monitoring works over SSH
- Notifications: Webhook notifications work regardless of locality
- Agent Tagging: Tags work the same locally and remotely

## Future Possibilities

- ntm ssh-setup: Help configure ControlMaster
- Connection pooling within ntm process
- Support for SSH jump hosts
- Parallel operations across multiple remotes

 {"id":"ntm-gon","title":"EPIC: Clipboard Integration","description":"Seamless copy/paste between agents and system clipboard.

## Problem Statement

Copying content from agents requires manual tmux operations:
1. Enter tmux copy mode (prefix + [)
2. Navigate to content start
3. Start selection
4. Navigate to content end
5. Copy to tmux buffer
6. Exit copy mode
7. Paste to system clipboard (separate step)

This friction discourages quick capture of agent outputs, code snippets,
and error messages. Users often retype or screenshot instead.

## Goals

1. One-command copy from any pane to system clipboard
2. Support filtering (last N lines, code blocks only, regex match)
3. Pipe clipboard contents to agents easily
4. Cross-platform clipboard support (macOS, Linux, WSL)

## Why This Matters

Fast copy/paste enables:
- Quick extraction of generated code
- Easy sharing of agent outputs
- Rapid iteration on prompts (copy response, modify, resend)
- Documentation capture
- Error reporting workflows

The goal is zero-friction capture: see something useful, capture it instantly.

## Success Criteria

- ntm copy myproject:1 copies pane 1 to clipboard
- ntm copy myproject --last 50 copies last 50 lines
- ntm copy myproject --pattern 'ERROR.*' copies matching lines
- ntm copy myproject:1 --code extracts code blocks only
- pbpaste | ntm send myproject --cc sends clipboard to agent
- Works on macOS (pbcopy), Linux (xclip/xsel), and WSL (clip.exe)

## Technical Approach

Platform Detection:
- macOS: pbcopy/pbpaste
- Linux X11: xclip -selection clipboard
- Linux Wayland: wl-copy/wl-paste
- WSL: clip.exe / powershell.exe Get-Clipboard
- Fallback: tmux's built-in clipboard with warning

Copy Pipeline:
1. Capture pane content (existing CapturePaneOutput)
2. Apply filters (line limit, regex, code extraction)
3. Pipe to platform clipboard command

Code Block Extraction:
- Parse markdown code fences from agent output
- Extract language hint if present
- Option to include/exclude fence markers
- Future: integrate with ntm-jar (Output Intelligence)

## Example Workflows

Capture last command output:
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --last 100

Capture only code blocks from agent response:
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --code
# Copies: function authenticate(token) { ... }

Send clipboard as prompt context:
$ pbpaste | ntm send myproject --cc 'Review this code:'

## Interaction with Other Epics

- Output Intelligence (ntm-jar): Advanced code extraction
- JSON Output: Clipboard content in JSON mode
- Remote Sessions: Clipboard is local, content comes from remote

## Design Decisions

Why shell out to clipboard commands?
- Cross-platform compatibility
- Leverages user's existing clipboard setup
- No CGo or native dependencies
- Works with clipboard managers

Why not integrate directly with tmux clipboard?
- Tmux buffer is separate from system clipboard
- Extra step to sync to system clipboard
- Direct approach is more user-friendly

## Future Possibilities

- ntm paste: Insert clipboard content into active pane
- Clipboard history integration
- Smart content detection (JSON, URL, file path)
- IDE integration (copy to editor's clipboard)

 {"id":"ntm-hat","title":"Implement state change ring buffer for delta tracking","description":"Track state changes over time to support delta snapshot queries.

## Rationale
--robot-snapshot --since needs to know what changed. This requires maintaining a history of state changes with timestamps.

## Design
Ring buffer of state change events:
```go
type StateChange struct {
    Timestamp time.Time
    Type      string // agent_output, agent_state, bead_update, mail_received, alert
    Session   string
    Pane      string // if applicable
    Details   map[string]interface{}
}

type StateTracker struct {
    changes []StateChange
    maxAge  time.Duration // prune changes older than this
    maxSize int           // max buffer size
    mu      sync.RWMutex
}
```

## Operations
- Record(change StateChange): Add new change
- Since(ts time.Time) []StateChange: Get changes since timestamp
- Coalesce(): Merge multiple output events into one summary

## Persistence
Consider optional file-based persistence for tracking across ntm invocations. Without persistence, delta tracking only works within single long-running session.

- Default max 1000 changes or 5 minutes of history

 {"id":"ntm-pp5","title":"EPIC: Prompt Management & History","description":"Track, replay, and template prompts for reproducibility and efficiency.

## Problem Statement
Every prompt sent via `ntm send` vanishes into the void:
- No record of what was sent, when, or to which agents
- Can't replay a successful prompt without retyping it
- No way to build a library of reusable prompts with variables
- Hard to reproduce multi-agent workflows across projects

## Goals
1. Log all prompts with full metadata (timestamp, session, targets, content)
2. Enable replay of previous prompts with optional modifications
3. Support named templates with variable substitution
4. Enable prompt sharing and reuse across projects

## Success Criteria
- `ntm history` shows chronological prompt log with filtering
- `ntm replay <id>` re-sends any historical prompt
- `ntm send --template=name` uses named template with variables
- Templates support {{file}}, {{selection}}, custom variables

## Technical Approach
- Store history in ~/.local/share/ntm/history.jsonl (append-only)
- Templates stored in ~/.config/ntm/templates/*.md
- Template syntax: {{variable}} with built-in and custom variables
- File content injection via --file flag

## Design Decisions
- JSONL for history (append-only, greppable, no DB needed)
- Markdown for templates (human-readable, easy to edit)
- Variable syntax matches common templating (Mustache-like)
- History is local-only (no sync) but exportable

## User Value
- Reproducible workflows for consistent results
- Faster iteration by replaying successful prompts
- Team knowledge sharing via template libraries
- Reduced typing for common operations","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:36:42.844416-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T02:36:42.844416-05:00"}
-{"id":"ntm-pp5.1","title":"Prompt History Logging","description":"Log all prompts sent via `ntm send` with full metadata.

## Overview
Every prompt sent through NTM should be recorded for later reference, replay, and analysis.

## Data Model
```go
type HistoryEntry struct {
    ID        string    `json:"id"`        // Unique ID (ulid or uuid)
    Timestamp time.Time `json:"ts"`        // When sent
    Session   string    `json:"session"`   // Session name
    Targets   []string  `json:"targets"`   // Pane names sent to
    Prompt    string    `json:"prompt"`    // Full prompt text
    Source    string    `json:"source"`    // "cli", "palette", "replay"
    Template  string    `json:"template"`  // Template name if used
    Success   bool      `json:"success"`   // Whether send succeeded
    Error     string    `json:"error"`     // Error message if failed
}
```

## Storage
Location: ~/.local/share/ntm/history.jsonl

Format: JSON Lines (one JSON object per line)
- Append-only for simplicity
- Greppable with standard tools
- Easy to backup/sync

```jsonl
{"id":"01HXYZ...","ts":"2025-12-10T10:30:00Z","session":"myproject","targets":["cc_1","cc_2"],"prompt":"implement auth","source":"cli","success":true}
{"id":"01HXYZ...","ts":"2025-12-10T10:31:00Z","session":"myproject","targets":["cod_1"],"prompt":"run tests","source":"palette","template":"run_tests","success":true}
```

## Integration Points

### ntm send
```go
func runSend(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    // ... existing send logic ...
    
    entry := history.Entry{
        ID:        ulid.New(),
        Timestamp: time.Now(),
        Session:   sessionName,
        Targets:   targetPanes,
        Prompt:    promptText,
        Source:    "cli",
    }
    
    err := sendToPane(pane, promptText)
    entry.Success = err == nil
    if err \!= nil {
        entry.Error = err.Error()
    }
    
    history.Append(entry)
    return err
}
```

### Palette Send
Same pattern - log after send with source="palette"

## Rotation/Cleanup
- Default: keep last 10,000 entries
- `ntm history prune --keep=N` to manually clean
- Or: rotate files monthly (history-2025-12.jsonl)

## Privacy Considerations
- Prompts may contain sensitive data
- History is local-only (not synced)
- `ntm history clear` to wipe all history
- Consider: --no-history flag on send for sensitive prompts

## Files to Create
- internal/history/storage.go - JSONL append/read

+{"id":"ntm-pp5.1","title":"Prompt History Logging","description":"Log all prompts sent via `ntm send` with full metadata.

## Overview
Every prompt sent through NTM should be recorded for later reference, replay, and analysis.

## Data Model
```go
type HistoryEntry struct {
    ID        string    `json:"id"`        // Unique ID (ulid or uuid)
    Timestamp time.Time `json:"ts"`        // When sent
    Session   string    `json:"session"`   // Session name
    Targets   []string  `json:"targets"`   // Pane names sent to
    Prompt    string    `json:"prompt"`    // Full prompt text
    Source    string    `json:"source"`    // "cli", "palette", "replay"
    Template  string    `json:"template"`  // Template name if used
    Success   bool      `json:"success"`   // Whether send succeeded
    Error     string    `json:"error"`     // Error message if failed
}
```

## Storage
Location: ~/.local/share/ntm/history.jsonl

Format: JSON Lines (one JSON object per line)
- Append-only for simplicity
- Greppable with standard tools
- Easy to backup/sync

```jsonl
{"id":"01HXYZ...","ts":"2025-12-10T10:30:00Z","session":"myproject","targets":["cc_1","cc_2"],"prompt":"implement auth","source":"cli","success":true}
{"id":"01HXYZ...","ts":"2025-12-10T10:31:00Z","session":"myproject","targets":["cod_1"],"prompt":"run tests","source":"palette","template":"run_tests","success":true}
```

## Integration Points

### ntm send
```go
func runSend(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    // ... existing send logic ...
    
    entry := history.Entry{
        ID:        ulid.New(),
        Timestamp: time.Now(),
        Session:   sessionName,
        Targets:   targetPanes,
        Prompt:    promptText,
        Source:    "cli",
    }
    
    err := sendToPane(pane, promptText)
    entry.Success = err == nil
    if err \!= nil {
        entry.Error = err.Error()
    }
    
    history.Append(entry)
    return err
}
```

### Palette Send
Same pattern - log after send with source="palette"

## Rotation/Cleanup
- Default: keep last 10,000 entries
- `ntm history prune --keep=N` to manually clean
- Or: rotate files monthly (history-2025-12.jsonl)

## Privacy Considerations
- Prompts may contain sensitive data
- History is local-only (not synced)
- `ntm history clear` to wipe all history
- Consider: --no-history flag on send for sensitive prompts

```go
func runSend(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    // ... existing setup ...
    
    // Prepare history entry
    entry := history.HistoryEntry{
        ID:        history.NewID(),
        Timestamp: time.Now(),
        Session:   sessionName,
        Prompt:    prompt,
        Source:    "cli",
    }
    
    // Collect targets as we send
    var targets []string
    var sendErr error
    
    for _, pane := range selectedPanes {
        targets = append(targets, pane.Title)
        if err := sendToPane(pane, prompt); err \!= nil {
            sendErr = err
            // Don't break - continue to other panes
        }
    }
    
    // Complete entry
    entry.Targets = targets
    entry.Success = sendErr == nil
    if sendErr \!= nil {
        entry.Error = sendErr.Error()
    }
    
    // Log to history (ignore errors - don't fail send for history)
    if err := history.Append(entry); err \!= nil {
        // Log warning but don't fail
        fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Warning: failed to log to history: %v\n", err)
    }
    
    return sendErr
}

 {"id":"ntm-pp5.2","title":"Prompt Replay Command","description":"New `ntm replay` command to re-send historical prompts.

## Command Interface
```bash
ntm replay 5                     # Re-send prompt #5 (from history)
ntm replay 01HXYZ...             # Re-send by ID
ntm replay --last                # Re-send most recent prompt
ntm replay 5 --to=cod            # Send to different targets
ntm replay 5 --session=other     # Send to different session
ntm replay 5 --edit              # Edit before sending
ntm replay 5 --dry-run           # Show what would be sent
```

## Lookup Behavior
1. If arg is a number, treat as index (1 = most recent)
2. If arg is a string, treat as ID prefix (match start)
3. `--last` is sugar for index 1

## Example Flow
```
$ ntm history
 #  TIME        SESSION      TARGETS         PROMPT (truncated)
 1  10:31:00    myproject    cc_1,cc_2       implement JWT authentication...
 2  10:30:00    myproject    cod_1           run all tests and fix failures
 3  10:25:00    otherproject gmi_1           analyze this codebase for...

$ ntm replay 1
Re-sending to myproject (cc_1, cc_2):
  "implement JWT authentication..."
Sent to 2 agents.

$ ntm replay 2 --to=cc --session=myproject
Re-sending to myproject (cc_1, cc_2):
  "run all tests and fix failures"
Sent to 2 agents.
```

## Edit Mode
When --edit is specified:
1. Write prompt to temp file
2. Open in 
3. Read modified content
4. Confirm and send

```go
func editPrompt(original string) (string, error) {
    f, _ := os.CreateTemp("", "ntm-prompt-*.md")
    f.WriteString(original)
    f.Close()
    
    editor := os.Getenv("EDITOR")
    if editor == "" { editor = "vim" }
    
    cmd := exec.Command(editor, f.Name())
    cmd.Stdin = os.Stdin
    cmd.Stdout = os.Stdout
    cmd.Run()
    
    modified, _ := os.ReadFile(f.Name())
    return string(modified), nil
}
```

## Flags
| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| --last | Replay most recent prompt |
| --to | Override targets (--cc, --cod, --gmi, or pane names) |
| --session | Send to different session |
| --edit | Edit prompt before sending |
| --dry-run | Show what would be sent |
| --no-history | Don't log this replay |

 {"id":"ntm-pp5.3","title":"Prompt Templates with Variables","description":"Named templates with variable substitution for reusable prompts.

## Overview
Templates are named, parameterized prompts stored in files.
Users can invoke them via `ntm send --template=name`.

## Storage Locations
1. Global: ~/.config/ntm/templates/*.md
2. Project: .ntm/templates/*.md (if Per-Project Config enabled)

## Template Format
```markdown
---
name: code_review
description: Review code for quality issues
variables:
  file: File path to review
  focus: What to focus on (optional)
---

Review the file {{file}} for:
- Code quality issues
- Potential bugs
- Performance concerns
{{#focus}}
Focus especially on: {{focus}}
{{/focus}}

Be thorough but concise.
```

## Variable Types

### Required Variables
`{{variable}}` - Must be provided via --var

### Optional Variables (with conditionals)
`{{#variable}}...{{/variable}}` - Section only included if variable provided

### Built-in Variables
| Variable | Value |
|----------|-------|
| `{{file}}` | Content of --file argument |
| `{{files}}` | Content of all --file arguments |
| `{{selection}}` | Current editor selection (if available) |
| `{{clipboard}}` | Clipboard content |
| `{{cwd}}` | Current working directory |
| `{{session}}` | Session name |
| `{{date}}` | Current date |
| `{{time}}` | Current time |

## Command Interface
```bash
# Use template
ntm send myproject --template=code_review --var file=src/auth.py

# Use with file injection
ntm send myproject -t code_review --file=src/auth.py
# {{file}} auto-populated with file contents

# List available templates
ntm template list

# Show template content
ntm template show code_review

# Create new template
ntm template create my_template
# Opens editor with scaffold
```

## Template Resolution
1. Look in project templates (.ntm/templates/)
2. Look in global templates (~/.config/ntm/templates/)
3. Look in built-in templates (embedded in binary)

## Built-in Templates
Ship with sensible defaults:
- code_review: Review code for issues
- explain: Explain how code works
- refactor: Refactor code
- test: Write tests
- document: Add documentation

## Parsing Implementation
```go
func ParseTemplate(content string, vars map[string]string) (string, error) {
    // Parse frontmatter
    meta, body := splitFrontmatter(content)
    
    // Check required variables
    for _, v := range meta.Variables {
        if _, ok := vars[v.Name]; \!ok && v.Required {
            return "", fmt.Errorf("missing required variable: %s", v.Name)
        }
    }
    
    // Expand conditionals
    body = expandConditionals(body, vars)
    
    // Substitute variables
    body = substituteVariables(body, vars)
    
    return body, nil
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-qxp","title":"Implement command plugins as executable scripts","description":"Allow users to add custom commands via executable scripts.

## What This Task Does

Enable users to create custom NTM commands by placing executable
scripts in ~/.config/ntm/commands/. Any executable becomes a subcommand.

## Plugin Location

~/.config/ntm/commands/
├── sync-all*         # ntm sync-all
├── morning-setup*    # ntm morning-setup  
└── deploy*           # ntm deploy

## Script Interface

Scripts receive NTM context via environment variables:
- NTM_SESSION: Current/specified session name
- NTM_JSON: Set to '1' if --json flag passed
- NTM_CONFIG_PATH: Path to NTM config file
- NTM_VERSION: NTM version string

Scripts can use ntm commands internally:
#\!/bin/bash
# ~/.config/ntm/commands/sync-all
for session in $(ntm list --json | jq -r '.[].name'); do
  ntm send "$session" --all "git pull && npm install"
done

## Command Discovery

On startup:
1. Scan ~/.config/ntm/commands/
2. Check each file is executable
3. Register as cobra subcommand
4. Extract description from script header comment

Script description convention:
#\!/bin/bash
# Description: Sync all agents with latest code
# Usage: ntm sync-all [--force]

## Integration with Help

$ ntm --help
...
Plugin Commands:
  sync-all      Sync all agents with latest code
  morning-setup Set up morning development session
  deploy        Deploy current branch to staging

$ ntm sync-all --help
# Runs: script --help

## Arguments and Flags

Pass through all arguments to script:
$ ntm deploy --env staging --force
# Runs: ~/.config/ntm/commands/deploy --env staging --force

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Executables in commands/ become subcommands
- [ ] Environment variables passed to scripts
- [ ] Arguments passed through
- [ ] Description parsed from header
- [ ] Help shows plugin commands
- [ ] Non-executable files ignored with warning

 {"id":"ntm-rlp","title":"Implement alert generation and tracking","description":"Generate actionable alerts from state analysis.

## Rationale
Alerts surface problems proactively. Instead of polling and analyzing raw state, the orchestrator gets pre-computed alerts.

## Alert Types
1. **Agent Stuck**: No output for N minutes
2. **Agent Crashed**: Pane process exited
3. **High CPU**: Agent consuming excessive resources
4. **Disk Low**: Running low on disk space
5. **Bead Stale**: In-progress bead with no activity
6. **Mail Backlog**: Many unread messages
7. **Dependency Cycle**: Bead dependency loop detected

## Alert Structure
```go
type Alert struct {
    ID        string    // unique alert ID
    Type      string    // agent_stuck, disk_low, etc.
    Severity  string    // info, warning, error, critical
    Message   string    // human-readable description
    Context   map[string]interface{} // relevant data
    CreatedAt time.Time
    ResolvedAt *time.Time
}
```

## Alert Lifecycle
1. Condition detected → Alert created
2. Alert included in snapshot/health output
3. Condition resolved → Alert marked resolved
4. Resolved alerts pruned after configurable time

## Configurable Thresholds
Via config file:
```toml
[alerts]
agent_stuck_minutes = 5
disk_low_threshold_gb = 5
mail_backlog_threshold = 10
